source_paper_title: On the Equivalence of Zero-Sum Games and Conic Programs
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Dimou_2026, title={On the Equivalence of Zero-Sum Games and Conic Programs}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2025.0926}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2025.0926}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Dimou, Nikos}, year={2026}, month=mar }
context:
Section 7 (Future directions), page 34 (PDF page labeled 34/37)

For semidefinite programming in particular, the decision of feasibility in polynomial time is a fundamental problem (Pataki and Touzov [35]).
title: Deciding semidefinite program feasibility in polynomial time via conic-game constructions
background_latex:
A (primal) semidefinite program (SDP) is a conic linear program over the cone of positive semidefinite matrices.
Let \(\mathbb{S}^m\) denote the real vector space of \(m\times m\) symmetric matrices, with Frobenius inner product \(\langle U,V\rangle := \mathrm{tr}(UV)\). Let \(\mathbb{S}^m_+\) be the closed convex cone of positive semidefinite matrices.

Given matrices \(A_1,\dots,A_n\in \mathbb{S}^m\), a cost matrix \(C\in \mathbb{S}^m\), and a right-hand side vector \(b\in \mathbb{R}^n\), the standard primal-dual SDP pair is
\[
\text{(P-SDP)}\quad \inf_{X\in \mathbb{S}^m} \ \langle C,X\rangle \ \text{s.t.}\ \langle A_i,X\rangle \ge b_i\ (i=1,\dots,n),\ \ X\succeq 0,
\]
\[
\text{(D-SDP)}\quad \sup_{y\in \mathbb{R}^n} \ b^\top y \ \text{s.t.}\ C-\sum_{i=1}^n y_i A_i \succeq 0,\ \ y\ge 0.
\]
Feasibility of \(\text{(P-SDP)}\) means there exists \(X\succeq 0\) with \(\langle A_i,X\rangle\ge b_i\) for all \(i\). Feasibility of \(\text{(D-SDP)}\) means there exists \(y\ge 0\) with \(C-\sum_i y_i A_i\succeq 0\).

A point \(X\) is strictly feasible for \(\text{(P-SDP)}\) if \(X\succ 0\) and \(\langle A_i,X\rangle>b_i\) for all \(i\). Analogously, \(y\) is strictly feasible for \(\text{(D-SDP)}\) if \(y>0\) and \(C-\sum_i y_i A_i\succ 0\). Existence of strictly feasible solutions (a Slater-type condition) is a common sufficient condition for strong duality and good algorithmic behavior.

The paper establishes a game-theoretic reformulation of strict feasibility for general conic programs and, specializing to SDPs, notes a connection to a known open question: whether feasibility of semidefinite programs can be decided in polynomial time.

The reformulation uses a two-player zero-sum game whose strategy sets are bases of cones and whose payoff is bilinear \(u(X,y)=\langle y, A(X)\rangle\); for SDPs one may take player I's strategies in a base of \(\mathbb{S}^m_+\) (e.g., \(\{X\succeq 0:\mathrm{tr}(X)=1\}\)) and player II's strategies in a base of \(\mathbb{R}^n_+\) (e.g., the simplex \(\{y\ge 0: \mathbf{1}^\top y=1\}\)). The paper states that determining strict feasibility for an SDP pair in polynomial time is equivalent (via its almost-equivalence theorem) to being able to construct, in polynomial time, an associated semidefinite game with certain properties (nonzero value, or zero value plus a null-set condition).
problem_definition_latex:
Design a polynomial-time algorithm that, given SDP data \((A_1,\dots,A_n,C,b)\), decides feasibility of semidefinite programming.

Concretely: given \(A_1,\dots,A_n,C\in \mathbb{S}^m\) and \(b\in \mathbb{R}^n\), determine in time polynomial in the input size whether \(\text{(P-SDP)}\) has a feasible solution \(X\succeq 0\) satisfying \(\langle A_i,X\rangle\ge b_i\) for all \(i\) (equivalently, whether the primal-dual SDP pair is feasible).

Equivalently (as highlighted by the paper): determine strict feasibility for the SDP pair in polynomial time; by the paper's almost-equivalence framework, this is equivalent to being able to construct in polynomial time an associated semidefinite zero-sum game (via polynomial-time construction of appropriate bases of \(\mathbb{S}^m_+\) and \(\mathbb{R}^n_+\)) whose game value is nonzero, or whose game value is zero while a specified null-set condition holds.
latex:
A (primal) semidefinite program (SDP) is a conic linear program over the cone of positive semidefinite matrices.
Let \(\mathbb{S}^m\) denote the real vector space of \(m\times m\) symmetric matrices, with Frobenius inner product \(\langle U,V\rangle := \mathrm{tr}(UV)\). Let \(\mathbb{S}^m_+\) be the closed convex cone of positive semidefinite matrices.

\]
\[
\text{(D-SDP)}\quad \sup_{y\in \mathbb{R}^n} \ b^\top y \ \text{s.t.}\ C-\sum_{i=1}^n y_i A_i \succeq 0,\ \ y\ge 0.

The paper establishes a game-theoretic reformulation of strict feasibility for general conic programs and, specializing to SDPs, notes a connection to a known open question: whether feasibility of semidefinite programs can be decided in polynomial time.

The reformulation uses a two-player zero-sum game whose strategy sets are bases of cones and whose payoff is bilinear \(u(X,y)=\langle y, A(X)\rangle\); for SDPs one may take player I's strategies in a base of \(\mathbb{S}^m_+\) (e.g., \(\{X\succeq 0:\mathrm{tr}(X)=1\}\)) and player II's strategies in a base of \(\mathbb{R}^n_+\) (e.g., the simplex \(\{y\ge 0: \mathbf{1}^\top y=1\}\)). Design a polynomial-time algorithm that, given SDP data \((A_1,\dots,A_n,C,b)\), decides feasibility of semidefinite programming.

Concretely: given \(A_1,\dots,A_n,C\in \mathbb{S}^m\) and \(b\in \mathbb{R}^n\), determine in time polynomial in the input size whether \(\text{(P-SDP)}\) has a feasible solution \(X\succeq 0\) satisfying \(\langle A_i,X\rangle\ge b_i\) for all \(i\) (equivalently, whether the primal-dual SDP pair is feasible).

Equivalently (as highlighted by the paper): determine strict feasibility for the SDP pair in polynomial time; by the paper's almost-equivalence framework, this is equivalent to being able to construct in polynomial time an associated semidefinite zero-sum game (via polynomial-time construction of appropriate bases of \(\mathbb{S}^m_+\) and \(\mathbb{R}^n_+\)) whose game value is nonzero, or whose game value is zero while a specified null-set condition holds.
open_problem_quote: For semidefinite programming in particular, the decision of feasibility in polynomial time is a fundamental problem (Pataki and Touzov [35]).
subject_classification: conic optimization and semidefinite programming complexity
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question is about existence of a polynomial-time decision procedure in the worst-case (a complexity-theoretic problem), not about a conjectured inequality likely to fail on finite instances. Searching for counterexamples is not a well-defined approach here.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: There is no clear automatic fitness function for candidate algorithms that certifies polynomial-time correctness on all inputs. While one can test heuristics on random SDPs, that does not provide reliable progress toward a provably polynomial-time feasibility algorithm.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
